text: Robotic transanal fistula repair is a technique advanced robotic surgeons should possess in their armamentarium. The motion and degrees of freedom are unmatched compared with standard transanal surgery. This allows more complex surgery to be performed in a minimally invasive fashion. We present a case of a fifty-nine-year-old female with a history of diverticulitis complicated with a stricture in her right ureter. During her robotic sigmoid colectomy, the right ureter was intentionally divided and reimplanted into the bladder by urology. Two months after her ileostomy was reversed, she started experiencing back and flank pain. CT demonstrated air tracking to the right kidney from a colonic ureteral fistula.

Here is a slice of the CT showing air in the right kidney. Here is a slice of the CT demonstrating air in the bladder. A flexible sigmoidoscopy and cystoscopy was performed to demonstrate a fistula from our colorectal anastomosis to the distal ureteral stump that had been previously transected. Air would extravasate up the re-implanted ureter to the right kidney. This is the port configuration for the robot. The transanal port was placed. Patient was placed in the prone jackknife position. A rigid proctoscopy identified the fistula opening at seventeen centimeters. The robot was docked parallel to the operating room table.
The surgeon identifies the lesion and uses a 2-0 absorbable self-locking suture to over-sew it. The suturing is performed in a running fashion until the fistula opening is completely closed.

The patient is positioned in lithotomy for a cystoscopy procedure. The surgeon uses fibrin glue and electrocautery to obliterate the right ureteral stump and fistula. Post-procedure, the patient is started on a clear liquid diet.
Robotic surgery is useful when performing complex transanal procedures. The three hundred sixty degree range of motion, three dimensional views, and ergonomics greatly assist the surgeon.
